steps.dialogue: Let's continue to build on your knowledge about fractions equaling one.
Take a look at these lines. Please click each line that shows a fraction equaling 1.

prompt.text: Click each line that shows a fraction equaling 1.

step.dialogue: Try taking another look at the top and bottom numbers. What pattern do we notice?

metadata.description: Highlight the fractions 3/3 and 8/8 showing matching numerators and denominators

step.dialogue: Try taking another look. A fraction shows 1 whole when the [event:696aeb7b9b658b6f]numerator and denominator are equal.

Can we work through this one together? [fraction numerator=3 denominator=3]Three thirds[/fraction] means [event:696aec6a0784de58]3 parts out of 3 total. That's all of them, so it equals 1. Same with [fraction numerator=8 denominator=8]eight eighths[/fraction]. All [event:696aecf194382e09]8 parts out of 8. But [fraction numerator=5 denominator=4]five fourths[/fraction] means [event:696af53097f67b5e] 5 parts when we only [event:696af57cf611ae38] need 4 for a whole. That's more than 1. This will come more easily the more we try these problems.

on_correct.dialogue: Good. [event:696ae8e351bf4780]You recognized that the matching numbers mean that [fraction numerator=3 denominator=3]three thirds[/fraction] and [fraction numerator=8 denominator=8]eight eighths[/fraction] both equal 1.

steps.dialogue: This time, we have a line with sixth marks. Which fraction with the denominator 6 equals 1?

prompt.text: Select the fraction with denominator 6 that equals 1.

step.dialogue: Let's count the jumps, where do six sixths take you?

metadata.description: Highlight the counting from 0 to 1 in sixths

step.dialogue: Six [event:696af6999752be9c] equal parts, all together, equals what?

We can work this one out together: each sixth is one equal jump. [event:696af7f5fe8866b1][fraction numerator=1 denominator=6]One sixth[/fraction], [event:696af87045fd9a24][fraction numerator=2 denominator=6]two sixths[/fraction], [event:696af897fdd11244][fraction numerator=3 denominator=6]three sixths[/fraction], [event:696af8aec5976fa8][fraction numerator=4 denominator=6]four sixths[/fraction], [event:696af8bdc6c62606][fraction numerator=5 denominator=6]five sixths[/fraction], and [event:696af8c8d57cfca9][fraction numerator=6 denominator=6]six sixths[/fraction] lands exactly at 1. [event:696af91738d7b3d3]Six equal parts make one whole. Nice. You're learning this concept.

on_correct.dialogue: [event:696af5d5cf4f05e1]Right. [fraction numerator=6 denominator=6]Six sixths[/fraction] equals [event:696e74eea2bbafcb] 1. You're using the pattern we worked on earlier.

steps.dialogue: If this point is [fraction numerator=2 denominator=4]two fourths[/fraction], what fraction comes two jumps after it?

step.dialogue: From [fraction numerator=2 denominator=4]two fourths[/fraction], count two more fourths.
[fraction numerator=2 denominator=4]two fourths[/fraction] plus two [event:696afb2363f8ca0d] more fourths equals what?

Let's count together: [event:696afb561514a86d][fraction numerator=2 denominator=4]two fourths[/fraction], [event:696afb718f09c33e]then [fraction numerator=3 denominator=4]three fourths[/fraction], [event:696afba146819224]then [fraction numerator=4 denominator=4]four fourths[/fraction]. [event:696afc13f33f63f3]That's two jumps after [fraction numerator=2 denominator=4]two fourths[/fraction].

on_correct.dialogue: Right. Two more fourths [event:696afa72d127d587] from [fraction numerator=2 denominator=4]two fourths[/fraction] gets us to [fraction numerator=4 denominator=4]four fourths[/fraction].

steps.dialogue: You understand that fractions [event:69700a018d56c4df]equal 1 when the top and bottom numbers match. We can put this knowledge to work in even more ways.
